principles.name: Open with your body or emotion, never the object
principles.how: The first line of the hook must contain I, my, or a feeling. If the first word is This, These, It, or a material/price, rewrite.
principles.why: Object-first openings train viewers to expect a product review; body-first openings promise a human story they cannot look away from.

principles.name: Replace specs with stakes in the first 10 seconds
principles.how: Delete all mentions of price, material, brand, or model from the first two sentences. Replace with the personal consequence if the test fails or succeeds.
principles.why: Specs create emotional distance; stakes create parasocial investment and a reason to care.

principles.name: Frame the video as a survival challenge, not a build log
principles.how: Reframe every build or test as a question about your body or life: Can I survive... / Will I break... instead of Will this work...
principles.why: Viewers retain human jeopardy; they swipe away from engineering iterations and process documentation.

principles.name: Use I wanted to see... instead of I designed... or I made...
principles.how: Change every I built/designed/printed to I decided to, I wanted to see if, or I set out to. Let the object be the method, never the subject.
principles.why: Agency and curiosity are universally relatable; craftsmanship is only interesting after the human hook is set.

principles.name: Insert a physical or emotional obstacle before explaining mechanics
principles.how: Before describing how anything works, state one thing that hurts, scares, exhausts, or limits you personally.
principles.why: Obstacles create narrative gravity; mechanics create cognitive load that triggers swipes.

principles.name: Describe internal sensations, not external adjustments
principles.how: In mid-script, write what your muscles, heart, lungs, or mind are doing. Delete sentences about redesigning, reprinting, or recalibrating unless framed as your frustration.
principles.why: Sensation is immersive; adjustment is boring because it removes the human witness from the action.

principles.name: Write hooks in breathless clauses under 25 words each
principles.how: Break every long sentence into two or three short fragments. Use periods instead of commas where possible.
principles.why: Fast pacing mirrors excitement and prevents the viewer from mentally checking out during complex explanations.

principles.name: Promise a human payoff, not a mechanical result
principles.how: End the hook by teasing a transformation in your body, relationships, or emotional state, not by showing the object functioning correctly.
principles.why: People share and finish videos for feelings, not functionality; mechanical payoffs feel like advertisements.

principles.name: Make failure hurt you, not just break the object
principles.how: When something fails, describe the physical pain, embarrassment, or lost opportunity you feel, not the broken part or design flaw.
principles.why: Personal cost makes failure a plot twist; broken parts make it a repair manual that viewers do not need.

principles.name: Include a paradox or counterintuitive claim in the title and hook
principles.how: Lead with something that sounds impossible, like gain weight in a minute. Avoid literal, descriptive titles that state exactly what the object is.
principles.why: Cognitive dissonance forces the brain to stay for resolution; literal descriptions let the brain assume it already knows the outcome.

principles.name: Use vulnerability as the primary driver
principles.how: State something you are bad at, scared of, or embarrassed by within the first 15 seconds. Lead with weakness, not competence.
principles.why: Perfection is unrelatable and intimidating; vulnerability creates parasocial bonds that make viewers root for you.

principles.name: Add a finite time limit or countdown to create urgency
principles.how: Put a hard deadline in the hook: in one minute, before the plane lands, within 24 hours.
principles.why: Open-ended tests feel like meandering vlogs; countdowns feel like events with inevitable consequences.

principles.name: Replace to test this with to see what would happen to me
principles.how: Search for the phrase to test and replace it with to find out if I could or to see how I would feel.
principles.why: Test is clinical and detached; see what happens is voyeuristic, personal, and irresistible.

principles.name: Ask a rhetorical question that puts the viewer in your shoes
principles.how: Use Have you ever wondered... or If you have ever... to bridge your experience to the viewer's imagination.
principles.why: Direct address converts passive scrolling into active imagination, which physically holds the thumb from swiping.

principles.name: Avoid inventory lists or step-by-step sequencing in hooks
principles.how: Delete Starting with X... or Step one... from the opening. Move procedural details to the mid-script and bury them inside action.
principles.why: Lists signal tutorial; narrative signals entertainment. Tutorials get saved and swiped; stories get watched.

principles.name: Use but or however to introduce emotional whiplash, not design pivots
principles.how: Every pivot word should change your emotional or physical state, not the engineering approach or materials.
principles.why: Emotional turns keep viewers locked in; technical pivots feel like delays that reward a swipe.

principles.name: Show messy bodily reality over polished prototypes
principles.how: Include blisters, vomit, sweat, tears, asthma attacks, or heartbreak in the hook or early script. Hide clean B-roll of the object.
principles.why: Messy reality is proof and spectacle; polish feels like a sponsored product review that viewers instinctively distrust.

principles.name: Make the object serve a personal story arc
principles.how: Ask what does this do TO me, not what does this do. Let every build step be motivated by a human need like winning a date or beating a fear.
principles.why: Tools are inherently boring; quests are inherently compelling. The object must be the obstacle or the weapon, not the protagonist.

principles.name: Use present-tense narration for immediacy
principles.how: Write as if it is happening now: My heart is pounding instead of My heart was pounding.
principles.why: Present tense collapses time and feels like live experience; past tense feels like a recap that can be skipped.

principles.name: Include social stakes involving other humans
principles.how: Mention another person who will judge, reward, humiliate, or date you based on the outcome.
principles.why: Social stakes multiply tension beyond personal willpower; solo tests feel like lonely hobbies.

principles.name: End every mid-script beat on a cliffhanger about your condition
principles.how: Finish each 30-second segment with But then I realized... or And that is when everything changed... referring to your body or emotions.
principles.why: Script cliffhangers reset attention spans at the exact moment they decay; object cliffhangers do not.

principles.name: Use single-sentence paragraphs for pacing
principles.how: Hit return after every sentence in the hook and mid-script. Never stack two long sentences without a visual or emotional break.
principles.why: Visual white space forces the mouth to speed up and the viewer to lean in, creating physical engagement.

principles.name: Prioritize what happened to me over how it works
principles.how: For every technical explanation, demand two sentences of personal consequence. If you cannot, cut the explanation entirely.
principles.why: Mechanism is the reward for retention, not the bait; viewers swipe when they feel lectured.

principles.name: Use absurd personal motivation rather than practical utility
principles.how: Motivate the build by a childish dream like being a superhero or going viral rather than efficiency, cost savings, or utility.
principles.why: Practicality is forgettable; absurdity is shareable because it signals entertainment value, not education.

principles.name: Eliminate cost, brand, and material mentions from hooks
principles.how: Move price and material names to the mid-script or omit them. Never let $20,000 or carbon fiber be the first descriptor.
principles.why: Cost framing makes the video about wealth and privilege; experience framing makes it about universal humanity.

principles.name: Use sensory details instead of functional descriptions
principles.how: Replace it generated 200 PSI with it felt like my legs were being crushed. Replace it burned through with it smelled like burning hair.
principles.why: Sensory data activates mirror neurons; specs and functional descriptions activate none.

principles.name: Create contrast between expectation and reality
principles.how: Explicitly state what you expected, then show the opposite. I thought it would be easy. I was horribly wrong.
principles.why: Violated expectations are the core of narrative satisfaction; meeting expectations is the core of boredom.

principles.name: Make the viewer root for your body, not the machine
principles.how: Write so that if the machine breaks, the viewer worries about YOU. If the machine works, the viewer celebrates YOU.
principles.why: Anthropomorphizing the creator beats anthropomorphizing the creation; people do not cry for broken plastic.

principles.name: Use I noticed or I realized to share discoveries intimately
principles.how: Frame discoveries as accidental personal observations rather than calculated engineering outcomes or planned tests.
principles.why: Noticing feels like friendship and authenticity; calculating feels like school or a sales pitch.

principles.name: Keep hooks under 60 words before the first twist or payoff
principles.how: Count the words in your hook. If you have not hit a problem, surprise, or visual payoff by word 60, cut everything before that point.
principles.why: The brain decides to swipe in under three seconds; density of events beats density of information.

principles.name: Introduce a new human problem in mid-script, not just a new engineering problem
principles.how: At the 30% and 60% marks, introduce a new physical limit or emotional low, not a new broken part or redesign.
principles.why: New engineering problems feel repetitive; new human limits feel escalating and prove the challenge is real.

principles.name: Use specific, body-centric numbers over abstract engineering figures
principles.how: Prefer 144 lbs, 200 beats per minute, or 33,000 steps to 200 PSI, 10,000 RPM, or 400 lb capacity.
principles.why: Body numbers are instantly relatable; engineering numbers are alienating and signal a dry tech video.

principles.name: Replace passive voice with I plus active verb plus feeling
principles.how: Change the plastic broke to I felt the plastic snap and thought I was going to fall.
principles.why: Passive voice removes the human witness; active voice demands empathy and creates a protagonist.

principles.name: Include a before/after of your emotional or physical state early
principles.how: In the first 20 seconds, show or describe what you looked or felt like before, and promise what you will look or feel like after.
principles.why: Transformation narratives are the oldest retention hack; they create a gap that demands closure.

principles.name: Escalate personal stakes rather than fixing the object
principles.how: When the first plan fails or succeeds, use I decided to take it to the next level to raise the human cost, not the technical complexity.
principles.why: Escalation signals that the video is still rising in intensity; fixes signal that the video is stuck in a loop.

principles.name: Avoid step one, step two language; use narrative time markers
principles.how: Replace First... Next... Then... with At first... But then... By the end... tied to your condition.
principles.why: Sequential language is instructional; temporal language tied to suffering is experiential.

principles.name: Make the goal personally impossible, not just technically difficult
principles.how: Frame the challenge as something your body or skill set is unfit for, like I have never played chess or I have asthma.
principles.why: Underdog positioning creates suspense; expert positioning creates expectation that you will succeed, removing tension.

principles.name: Use audio/visual sensory hooks in the title
principles.how: Include words like Sounds INSANE, THIS happened, or shocked-face emoji that promise a sensory or emotional reaction.
principles.why: Titles are billboards; they must sell a feeling, not a topic. Feelings stop thumbs.

principles.name: Close mid-script loops on your emotional state, not the object's state
principles.how: When resolving a scene, answer How did I feel? before Did it work?
principles.why: Emotional closure satisfies the viewer; functional closure only informs them, and information is not entertainment.

principles.name: Use a dare or bet energy
principles.how: Write with the voice of someone walking into a bar bet or a childhood dare, not a lab report or instruction manual.
principles.why: Bet energy is contagious and low-stakes fun; clinical energy is repellent because it asks the viewer to work.

principles.name: Make failure a story of your limits, not the object's limits
principles.how: When you fail, say I was not strong enough or I panicked, not the motor was not strong enough.
principles.why: Owning failure makes you the protagonist; blaming the object makes you a reviewer and the object the star.

principles.name: Reference death, injury, or heartbreak literally
principles.how: Use concrete words like die, break, bleed, pass out, or heartbreak rather than vague danger or risk.
principles.why: Literal stakes are neurologically arousing; abstract risk is filtered out as background noise.

principles.name: Alternate action beats with reflection beats
principles.how: After every physical action sentence, write one sentence about what you thought or felt in that moment.
principles.why: Reflection gives the viewer a breath and deepens character; nonstop action becomes monotonous noise.

principles.name: Name a specific fear or insecurity and confront it directly
principles.how: State my biggest fear is... or my weakest moment was... and make the challenge directly address that named vulnerability.
principles.why: Named fears are specific and memorable; unnamed challenges are generic and replaceable with any other video.

principles.name: Replace it worked with it made me feel
principles.how: When the object functions, describe your surprise, relief, terror, or pain rather than confirming the technical success.
principles.why: Feelings are the payoff; confirmation is anticlimactic because it ends curiosity without providing emotion.

principles.name: Answer So what? with a human cost in every mid-script beat
principles.how: After writing each mid-script section, ask So what? If the answer is about the object, rewrite it to be about your body, time, or relationships.
principles.why: So what is the viewer's internal swipe trigger; human cost is the defense that justifies watching.

principles.name: Use the second person to bridge experience
principles.how: Insert one if you have ever... or you probably think... per hook to directly activate the viewer's memory.
principles.why: Second person creates a conversational conspiracy between creator and viewer that feels like shared experience.

principles.name: Make the title a personal transformation, not an object capability
principles.how: Rewrite titles so they could be memoir chapters, not product pages. I Became... and What Happened to Me... beat Making... and Testing...
principles.why: Transformation titles promise identity change; capability titles promise instruction, and instruction is not binge-worthy.

principles.name: Hide the build process inside the struggle
principles.how: If you must mention printing, welding, or coding, embed it inside a sentence about your frustration, exhaustion, or deadline pressure.
principles.why: Process is tolerated only when it is the obstacle causing your pain; process as content is a retention killer.

principles.name: End the hook with a mystery about your condition, not the object's performance
principles.how: The final line of the hook should be And I had no idea what was about to happen to me, never And then the machine turned on.
principles.why: Mysteries about people are cliffhangers; mysteries about machines are instruction manuals that viewers swipe away from.
